title: Public trust in Facebook following the Cambridge Analytica data privacy scandal

polling_trajectory.source: Estimated: Baseline trust levels prior to full scandal impact based on 2017 trends
Estimated: Immediate reaction to initial reporting and Zuckerberg apology
Estimated: Gradual decline as privacy settings updates were rolled out
Estimated: Continued market fatigue regarding privacy news
Estimated: Final survey alignment with Pew Research findings

events.description: The New York Times and The Guardian break the Cambridge Analytica story
Mark Zuckerberg issues formal apology via Facebook post
Mark Zuckerberg testifies before the US Congress

agents.name: Mark Zuckerberg
agents.description: CEO of Facebook, primary defender of the platform's integrity

agents.name: Christopher Wylie
agents.description: Whistleblower who exposed the data harvesting practices

agents.name: US Congress
agents.description: Legislative body investigating data privacy and platform regulation

agents.name: Facebook Shareholders
agents.description: Investors concerned with stock price stability and regulatory risk

agents.name: Privacy Advocates
agents.description: Groups like EFF pushing for stricter data control

agents.name: General Facebook Users
agents.description: The average user base experiencing cognitive dissonance between utility and privacy concerns

notes: Pro-side (+1) is defined as users who continue to trust Facebook or maintain their account without significant changes. Against-side (-1) is defined as users who distrust the platform, deleted their account, or significantly restricted their data sharing. Data quality: Polling trajectory is estimated based on the Pew Research finding that while 51% of users took action to change privacy settings, the overall user base did not abandon the platform en masse.
